title: Design a Todo List API
problemStatement: Design a RESTful API for a simple todo list application. Users should be able to create, read, update, and delete todo items. Each todo has a title, description, status (pending/completed), and timestamps.
functionalRequirements: List all todos with optional status filter
constraints: Title is required and max 200 characters
Deleted todos should return 404 on subsequent access

rubric.reasoning: A complete CRUD API should expose all five operations. Missing any one means clients can't fully manage the resource. GET list + GET single are different: list returns paginated results, single returns the full resource.

GET = read (idempotent, safe), POST = create (not idempotent), PUT = full replace (idempotent), PATCH = partial update (not necessarily idempotent), DELETE = remove (idempotent). Using POST for everything is a common anti-pattern that loses these semantic guarantees.

Precise status codes let clients handle responses programmatically. Using 200 for everything forces clients to parse the body to detect errors. 201 vs 200 signals 'new resource created' vs 'existing resource returned'. 204 for DELETE says 'done, nothing to return.'

Every invalid input should produce a specific, actionable error. Generic '400 Bad Request' without details forces developers to guess. Include the field name, the constraint violated, and the valid options.
Consistent response shapes reduce client complexity. Always include the resource ID in responses, use camelCase consistently, and include timestamps (createdAt, updatedAt) for debugging and caching.
Any list endpoint without pagination is a ticking bomb. As data grows, unbounded queries will crash clients and servers. Include total count and page metadata so clients can build navigation UIs.

defendQuestions.question: You used PUT for updating todos. What's the difference between PUT and PATCH, and when would PATCH be more appropriate here?
defendQuestions.modelReasoning: PUT replaces the entire resource — the client must send ALL fields, even unchanged ones. If you omit a field, it gets set to null. PATCH sends only the changed fields. For a todo app, PATCH is arguably better: updating just the status shouldn't require re-sending the title and description. However, PUT is simpler to implement and reason about. In interviews, show you understand both and can justify your choice.

defendQuestions.question: Should DELETE /todos/:id permanently remove the todo from the database, or soft-delete it (set a 'deleted' flag)? What are the tradeoffs?
defendQuestions.modelReasoning: Hard delete: simpler, no orphaned data, 404 on re-access is clean. Soft delete: supports undo/restore, audit trails, and prevents accidental data loss. Tradeoff: soft delete requires filtering deleted records from all queries (easy to forget, causes data leaks). For a simple todo app, hard delete is fine. For production apps with compliance requirements, soft delete with a background job to purge after 30 days.

defendQuestions.question: Two users try to update the same todo simultaneously. How would your API handle this conflict?
defendQuestions.modelReasoning: Without concurrency control, the last write wins (silently overwriting the other). Solutions: (1) Optimistic concurrency with ETags — each response includes an ETag (version hash), and PUT/PATCH requires an If-Match header. If the ETag doesn't match, return 409 Conflict. (2) Last-write-wins with timestamps — simpler but loses data. For a todo app, optimistic concurrency is usually overkill, but knowing the pattern shows production experience.

endpoints.description: List all todos with optional filtering and pagination
Get a single todo by ID

designNotes: Return 201 with the created resource on POST
Return 204 No Content on successful DELETE
Include timestamps (createdAt, updatedAt) for audit trails
Support filtering by status on the list endpoint
